segments.s646-g9: فغانیش را گفت is a ditransitive-style phrasing meaning 'he said to his lament' or 'he addressed his lament/cry.' را marks the following noun as the (indirect) object/target of گفت. کای is a vocative 'O' calling someone, and نیک‌خواه is the addressee 'well-wisher' (a compound adjective used as a noun).
segments.s646-g10: دو فرزند بودیم is straight past-tense first-person plural 'we were two children/sons.' زیبای گاه is a poetic genitive/attributive phrase 'of the fair hour/time' (literally 'beautiful-of time'), describing the time or condition of their birth or being; the adjective comes after the noun in this older poetic order.
segments.s646-g11: پدر is the subject 'the father.' تاج شاهی is a genitive/construct 'the crown of kingship' (tāj + shāhī). به کهتر سپرد uses به + noun to mark the recipient 'to the lesser/younger one'; سپرد is the past tense 'he entrusted/handed over.'
segments.s646-g12: چو here functions temporally 'when' (or 'as'), introducing what follows. بیدادگر بد سپرد reads as 'the tyrant (bīdādgar), acting badly/ill, received/held it' (سپرد again as 'took/was given'); و بمرد is a terse clause 'and he died' — بمرد is an optative/past-formula meaning 'he died' (in poetry used as a narrative past or curse).
segments.s646-g13: چو لشکر دهی is a conditional 'if/when you give an army' with دهی as a present/subjunctive second-person singular 'you give.' مر مرا are older and newer forms of the dative/emphatic object 'to me' (a rhetorical doubling common in poetry), and گنج هست is a verbless/existential clause 'there is treasure' or 'I have treasure.'
segments.s646-g14: A simple asyndetic list: سلیح 'weapons', و 'and', بزرگی 'greatness' (or high rank), و 'and', نیرویِ دست 'the strength/power of the hand' — a genitive construction where نیروی governs دست ('the might of the hand'). The list completes what 'گنج هست' introduced (things he possesses).
segments.s646-g15: فغانی بدو گفت is 'the lamenter said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). که introduces the following clause/reported speech. آری رواست is an emphatic reply: آری 'indeed/yes' and رواست (روا + است contracted) 'it is fitting/permissible.'
segments.s646-g16: جهاندار 'sovereign' is the subject; هم 'also'/ 'too' adds emphasis; بر پدر is a prepositional phrase 'over/on/with respect to the father'; پادشاست is a fused predicate 'is king/monarch' (پادشاه + است). The clause states a maxim about a ruler's authority even with regard to his father.
segments.s646-g33: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' and is followed by the complementizer تا here meaning 'that' (not temporal 'until'), introducing a subordinate clause. بارگی is the noun phrase that functions as the place/thing to be taken (an archaic/poetic word for a seat/place to sit on) and برنشست is the simple past, 3rd person singular, 'sat upon' or 'took his seat' — so the structure is: 'He ordered that (someone) sit on the (baragī)'.
segments.s646-g34: بشد is a narrative past-form meaning 'it happened / he became' and takes the predicate adjective تیز 'swift/quick' (i.e. 'he became swift' / 'he moved quickly'). The conjunction و links this clause to the next. ببسود is an older/poetic verb form governing رویش (روی + pronominal suffix ش = 'his face') as its object; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in/by the hand' indicating the instrument. Together the clause describes a rapid action: 'and (he) seized/grasped his face with his hand.'
segments.s646-g35: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. بازش is باز 'back/again' plus the object suffix ش 'him', so 'sent him back'. به ایوان خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to his iwan/porch/hall' where خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' (here written as خوی + ش in the word list but functioning together). Word order is verb – object – prepositional phrase.
segments.s646-g36: بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him'. خوانده here functions as a past participle/verbal adjective 'calling/called' (i.e. 'having called' or 'calling him'), and its complement is بد عهد و پیمان خویش — a descriptive phrase where بد 'bad' modifies عهد و پیمان 'oath and pledge' and خویش (with the suffix ش in the word list) is the reflexive possessive 'his own'. Grammatically this segment reports how he sent him away while naming him 'bad/faithless in his oath and covenant.'
segments.s646-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. هرمز is the personal name (subject/context). برآمد is a past tense verb 'rose/ascended' (classical narrative past). به تخت پدر is a prepositional phrase of goal ('to/into the father's throne'), with به + noun; word order places the directional phrase after the verb in poetry but conceptually marks movement toward the throne.
segments.s646-g2: به سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head'. برنهاد is the past tense verb 'placed/raised on' (classical compound verb meaning 'put on'); the subject آن کیی ('that Kayanian') follows the verb in poetic inversion, and تاج زر is the direct object 'the golden crown' (tāj + genitive modifier زر).
segments.s646-g3: چو = 'when' (temporal). پیروز را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (marks Pirooz as the addressee/target). ویژه گفتی is 'spoke specially/privately' — ویژه modifies گفتی. گفتی is a past tense narrative form (classical/poetic past), here functioning as 'he said/spoke'. ز خشم is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of / out of anger' that explains motive.
segments.s646-g4: همی here is the classical progressive/inchoative adverb (roughly 'then/so/continually') preceding the noun phrase. آب رشک is a figurative compound 'water of envy' = 'tears of jealousy'. اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'came into/entered', with اندر 'into/in' plus آمد 'came'. به چشم is the directional target 'to the eye' (i.e. 'into his eye').
segments.s646-g5: سوی + noun = 'toward' or 'in the direction of' (here 'toward King Hital'). شد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb 'became/happened' used in narrative to indicate motion or change of state (here 'turned/moved toward'). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb phrase. The agent/pronoun is implicit (typical of epic Persian).
segments.s646-g6: This phrase lists what the subject brought/was accompanied by: (�)با لشکر و گنج و چندی مهان. In classical poetic spelling the pronoun + با can fuse (see explanation); functionally this introduces accompaniment 'with an army, treasure, and some guests'. The items are coordinated by و. Note: the head token 'ابا' here appears to be a poetic fusion (uo + ba = 'he with'), not the usual lemma meaning 'refusal'.
segments.s646-g7: This nominal phrase names/characterizes a person. چغانی is a nisba/adjectival name (indicating origin or clan). شهی is an adjective 'royal'. بد qualifies the following noun as 'bad/ill'. فغانیش is used here as a name or epithet related to 'foghān/lamen t' (so 'his lament' or 'lamentation' used as a name), and نام 'name' closes the predicate identifying that name. Word order is nominal and appositive in classical style.
segments.s646-g8: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeker/ambitious (one)'. با introduces accompaniment 'with' and governs the coordinated list لشکر و گنج و کام 'army and treasure and desire' (کام here = desire/wish). The whole segment qualifies the named person as being accompanied by force, wealth, and appetite.
segments.s646-g25: This is a compact, slightly inverted SOV clause: object 'سپاهی' (army/troops) comes first for emphasis, then the verb 'بیاورد' (past/present-past narrative) and finally the agent/title 'پیروزشاه' ('the victorious king'). In Ferdowsi's epic style the verb can appear before or after the subject; here the order highlights the army. 'بیاورد' is classical past narrative meaning 'brought/led (forth)'.
segments.s646-g26: 'که' introduces a subordinate/result clause ('that' / 'so that'). The phrase literally reads 'from dust dark became the wheel of the moon': 'از' = 'from', 'گرد' = 'dust', 'تاریک' = 'dark', 'شد' = 'became'. Word order is poetic: the predicate ('تاریک شد') precedes the subject ('چرخ ماه'), which is a common invertive emphasis in classical Persian; 'چرخ ماه' = 'the moon's disk/wheel'.
segments.s646-g27: 'برآویخت' is an elevated/poetic past verb meaning 'rose up' or 'made to attack/engage'. It is followed by the preposition 'با' + name/title 'هرمز شهریار' ('with / against Hormoz the king'); in context it denotes engagement in battle. The verb-first order is stylistic and common in epic narration.
segments.s646-g28: This clause reports the intensity of fighting. 'فراوان' is an adverb/adjectival idea 'abundant/many'. 'ببودستشان' is a fused classical form meaning roughly 'there was for them' / 'they had' (literally 'it was to their hand'), so the construction means 'there was abundant battle in their hands'—i.e., many battles were fought between them. 'کارزار' is the noun 'battle'.
segments.s646-g29: 'سرانجام' = 'finally' or 'in the end', an adverbial fronting the clause. 'هرمز گرفتار شد' is a simple past passive/affected-state: 'Hormoz was caught/captured' ('گرفتار' = 'captured/trapped' + 'شد' = 'became/was'). The construction is straightforward narrative past.
segments.s646-g30: Poetic clause meaning 'all crowns were made low before him.' 'همه تاج‌ها' = 'all crowns', 'پیش او' = 'in his presence / before him', 'خوار شد' = 'became humiliated/despised' (passive/result). The verb 'شد' finishes the predicate; word order places the locative/goal 'پیش او' before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s646-g31: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'پیروز' (here the victor/Pirooz) is the subject; 'روی برادر بدید' = 'saw (the) face of (his) brother'—object 'روی برادر' follows the subject and precedes the verb 'بدید' (past 'saw'). The clause is a standard temporal 'when X saw Y' construction in classical Persian.
segments.s646-g32: This is the main clause: 'دلش' = 'his heart' (possessive clitic '-ش' = 'his') is the logical subject/experiencer; 'مهر پیوند او' is the complex object/group — 'مهر' = 'love/affection', modified by 'پیوند او' = 'the bond/link with him' (so 'love of union with him' or 'love as a bond to him'); 'برگزید' = 'chose/selected' is the past verb at the end. Grammatically, 'او' links to 'پیوند' (the person with whom the bond is made).
segments.s646-g17: به پیمان (literally “by/oath”) places the action under a pact or oath; سپارم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/intentional 'I entrust' (classical Persian uses this form for a promise or vow). The following سپاهی تو را is the thing entrusted: سپاهی (army/troops) is the direct object and تو را (‘you’ + object marker) marks the recipient; word order (verb before object and recipient) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s646-g18: نمایم is 1st-person verb 'I will show/send/make (go)' (here with the sense 'send/make go'); سوی داد راهی is a prepositional phrase 'toward the path of justice' (سوی = toward; داد = 'justice' acting as a noun modifying راهی 'path/route'); تو را again marks the person affected ('you' as direct object). The clause as a whole expresses intent to send/lead the addressee toward justice.
segments.s646-g19: که introduces a subordinate optative clause 'that … may be'; باشد is the 3rd-person subjunctive/optative 'may be' (classical usage expressing desire/possibility). مرا (here split in the wordlist as م + را) is the object pronoun 'for me' (meaning 'be mine'), and ترمذ و ویسه‌گرد are the locations/names that are the things hoped to belong to the speaker. Note: in the supplied tokenization 'م' and 'را' are separated; together they form 'مرا' = 'to/for me'.
segments.s646-g20: که again begins an explanatory clause 'for/since'; خون عهد این = 'this blood-oath/pledge' (خون + عهد = a blood-bound covenant) with این 'this' specifying it. دارم is 1st-person present 'I have/hold' (the speaker asserts possession of that pledge), and از یزدگرد specifies the source 'from Yazdegerd' (از = from). The clause explains the title to those lands — the speaker claims it by a blood-pledge received from the king.
segments.s646-g21: بدو = 'to him' (dative/indirect object). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here reported speech). پیروز is the speaker's name (Piruz) placed close to the speech verb. کآری رواست is a short reported clause: ک (that) + آری ('indeed/yes' in older/poetic usage) and رواست (روا + ست: adjective 'fitting/permissible' + copula 'is') = 'that indeed is fitting.' The whole segment reports Piruz's reply/agreement.
segments.s646-g22: فزون زان is a connective 'moreover/greater than that / besides, for that reason' (فزون = 'more/besides', زان = 'from that/therefore'); به تو = 'to you/for you' marking beneficiary; پادشاهی = 'kingship/kingdom' (noun); سزاست is a present impersonal construction 'it is deserved' (سزا + است = 'is deserved'). Together: 'furthermore, because of that you deserve the kingship.'
segments.s646-g23: بدو = 'to him' (dative). داد is the past 'gave' (transitive). شمشیرزن سی‌هزار is a noun phrase 'thirty thousand swordsmen' (شمشیرزن head noun 'swordsman' with the numeral following in poetic order). The clause states the gift/payment: 'he gave him thirty thousand swordsmen.'
segments.s646-g24: ز = 'from' (poetic form of از); هیتالیان names the people/clan/region ('the Hitalian(s)'); لشکری = 'army/troops' used as a noun describing the force; نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/illustrious.' The phrase qualifies the troops just given: 'a renowned army from the Hitalians.'

sentences.s646-s5: He said to his lament, "O well-wisher, we were two children born in a fair hour."
sentences.s646-s6: The father entrusted the royal crown to the lesser one; when the cruel tyrant received it he acted ill and died.
sentences.s646-s7: If you give me an army, I have treasure — weapons, greatness, and the strength of hand.
sentences.s646-s8: The lamenter said to him, 'Yes — it is fitting; a sovereign, too, is a king over his father.'
sentences.s646-s17: He ordered that he sit on the seat; then he quickly sprang forward and seized his face with his hand.
sentences.s646-s18: He sent him back to his own iwan, calling him faithless to his oath and pledge.
sentences.s646-s1: When Hormoz ascended to his father's throne, that Kayanian set the golden crown upon his head.
sentences.s646-s2: When he spoke privately to Pirooz out of anger, the water of envy came into his eye.
sentences.s646-s3: Suddenly he turned toward King Hital, with an army and treasure and some guests.
sentences.s646-s4: The Chaghani — a royal one, ill famed for lament — named Faghanish, a world-seeker with army, treasure, and desire.
sentences.s646-s13: The victorious king brought an army, so that the moon's disk grew dark from the dust.
sentences.s646-s14: He rose up against Hormoz the king; there was abundant fighting in their hands.
sentences.s646-s15: At last Hormoz was captured; all crowns were humbled before him.
sentences.s646-s16: When Pirooz saw his brother's face, his heart chose love as a bond to him.
sentences.s646-s9: I will, by covenant, entrust an army to you and send you toward the path of justice.
sentences.s646-s10: That Termez and Visehgird may be mine; I hold this blood-pledge from Yazdegerd.
sentences.s646-s11: Piruz said to him, 'Yes, that is fitting; moreover, because of that you deserve the kingship.'
sentences.s646-s12: He gave him thirty thousand swordsmen, a renowned host from the Hitalians.
